title: Using Inflation Shock Patterns to Help Forecast Inflation - San Francisco Fed
gist: SF Fed researchers introduce the Inflation Shock Momentum Index (ISMI), which tracks the share of PCE spending categories running three consecutive same-signed monthly inflation surprises to detect emerging inflationary or disinflationary pressure in real time. The index improves PCE inflation forecasts at one-to-three-year horizons, and as of mid-2026 it is oscillating around zero, signaling inflation may stay near current elevated levels.

themes.theme: The ISMI indicator and its construction

insights.claim: The Inflation Shock Momentum Index (ISMI) is a new real-time indicator that measures the expenditure-weighted share of PCE spending categories with positive inflation momentum minus the share with negative momentum, to signal emerging inflationary or disinflationary pressure.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Gives investors a free, monthly-updated, direction-aware gauge of whether underlying inflation pressure is building or fading, ahead of headline prints.
insights.quote: The ISMI represents the expenditure-weighted share of inflation categories with positive momentum minus the share with negative momentum.
insights.locator: This Economic Letter describes a new indicator

insights.claim: A PCE spending category is classified as having positive momentum after three consecutive positive monthly inflation shocks and negative momentum after three consecutive negative shocks, where a shock is the gap between observed monthly inflation and a rolling 120-month trend model's prediction.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Defines exactly how the signal is triggered, so an investor can anticipate when categories flip and the index turns.
insights.quote: The difference between the observed monthly inflation rate and the fitted trend model’s prediction defines the inflation shock.
insights.locator: For each of the 130 or so categories

insights.claim: The three-consecutive-month classification rule was chosen deliberately because a two-month rule is more likely to be triggered by chance while a four-month rule excludes many informative signals, though results are robust to alternative rules.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Signals methodological robustness, reducing the risk that the index is an artifact of arbitrary parameter choices.
insights.quote: A two-month rule is more likely to be satisfied by chance, while a four-month rule may exclude many informative signals.
insights.locator: We then examine the most recent sequence

insights.claim: The ISMI is built by sorting roughly 130 categories of the PCE basket and estimating a simple trend model per category (regressing monthly inflation on a constant and its 1-month lag over a rolling 120-month window).
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: The granular category-level approach extracts more inflation information per month than aggregate measures, which matters for early detection.
insights.locator: For each of the 130 or so categories

insights.claim: Since 1969, on average about 20% of monthly category inflation rates exhibit three consecutive positive shocks while about 15% exhibit three consecutive negative shocks, giving the ISMI a slight structural positive baseline.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Establishes the neutral baseline; readings meaningfully above the ~5-point positive tilt flag genuine upward pressure.
insights.quote: around 20% of the total monthly inflation rates across categories exhibit three consecutive positive shocks, while around 15% exhibit three consecutive negative shocks
insights.locator: The ISMI therefore identifies the direction

insights.claim: Inflation can be decomposed into a slow-moving historical trend plus new unexpected shocks, and it stays persistently high or low either because the trend is slow to change or because the shocks themselves are long-lasting.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Frames why single-cause inflation models fail; investors should watch both trend shifts and shock durability.
insights.quote: inflation can stay high or low for two reasons: (1) The underlying inflation trend is slow to change, or (2) the underlying shocks can be long lasting

insights.claim: Unlike existing persistence indicators that merely gauge overall persistence, the ISMI distinguishes upward from downward inflation pressure and uses only recent data for early detection of regime shifts.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Directionality plus recency is the edge over conventional inflation-persistence measures for timing trades.
insights.quote: it distinguishes between upward versus downward inflation pressure, rather than simply gauging overall persistence

insights.claim: Delayed recognition of persistent inflation risks monetary policy falling behind the curve and un-anchoring inflation expectations, whereas premature tightening risks output or employment losses.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: The symmetric policy-error framing explains why the Fed values early-warning tools like the ISMI in setting rate timing.
insights.quote: A delay in recognizing persistent inflationary pressures raises the risk that monetary policy will fall behind the curve

insights.claim: The ISMI was persistently positive during the Great Inflation of the late 1970s and early 1980s, then turned sharply negative through the subsequent disinflation and into the 1990s, matching known inflation history.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Historical alignment with major regimes builds confidence the signal is real, not curve-fit.
insights.quote: The index is persistently positive during the Great Inflation era of the late 1970s and early 1980s.

insights.claim: PCE inflation fell from around 5% in 1990 to 2% in 1999, the period when long-run inflation expectations became well-anchored to the Fed's longer-term goal.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Anchoring of expectations coincided with a sustained negative ISMI, a template for recognizing durable disinflation.
insights.quote: PCE inflation declined from around 5% in 1990 to 2% in 1999.

insights.claim: From June 2009 until the pandemic's start in February 2020, 12-month headline PCE inflation was below the Fed's 2% goal for 101 of 129 months (78% of the time), coinciding with a mostly negative ISMI.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Quantifies the decade-long below-target regime and shows the ISMI tracked it, useful for gauging persistent low-inflation environments.
insights.quote: 12-month headline PCE inflation was below the Fed’s 2% goal for 101 out of 129 months, or 78% of the time

insights.claim: During the pandemic the ISMI first fell at the 2020 recession's onset on widespread negative demand shocks, then rose rapidly in 2021-2022 as PCE inflation surged to a 40-year high of 7.24% in June 2022.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: The index captured the demand-shock-to-inflation-surge sequence, validating its use in fast-moving regimes.
insights.quote: PCE inflation surged to a 40-year high of 7.24% in June 2022

insights.claim: After the 2022 inflation peak the ISMI declined faster than PCE inflation and turned negative, signaling broad disinflationary pressure even while PCE inflation remained above the Fed's 2% goal.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: The index can flag disinflation before headline inflation confirms it, a leading-indicator edge for rate-cut positioning.
insights.quote: the ISMI declines faster than PCE inflation and eventually turns negative

insights.claim: The ISMI improves out-of-sample PCE inflation forecasts, reducing forecast errors by approximately 5 to 10%, with larger improvement at the two-to-three-year horizons.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: A measurable forecasting edge at multi-year horizons is directly useful for positioning duration and inflation-linked assets.
insights.quote: including the ISMI in the out-of-sample forecasting model reduces forecast errors by approximately 5 to 10%

insights.claim: The greater forecast gain at longer horizons suggests the ISMI detects slow-moving structural shifts in the inflation process that standard predictors miss.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Positions the ISMI as a structural, not just cyclical, inflation signal for long-horizon macro views.
insights.quote: the ISMI can detect slow-moving structural shifts in the underlying inflation process that other standard predictors may miss

insights.claim: A one-standard-deviation surprise increase in the ISMI is associated with a 0.5 percentage point cumulative rise in the PCE price index after 36 months, controlling for Michigan inflation expectations, the vacancy-to-unemployment ratio, and oil prices.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Quantifies the pass-through from an ISMI surprise to future price levels beyond standard predictors.
insights.quote: A surprise increase in the ISMI is associated with a 0.5 percentage point cumulative increase in the PCE price index after 36 months

insights.claim: The negative-momentum component of the ISMI has a larger predictive impact than the positive component, making the index especially useful for detecting future disinflationary pressures.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: The index is a sharper tool for calling disinflation than inflation, informing when to lean into rate-cut or long-duration trades.
insights.quote: the ISMI is particularly useful for detecting future disinflationary pressures

insights.claim: Forecast validity is established using LASSO in an out-of-sample exercise that uses no future inflation data, approximating real-time forecasting conditions.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Real-time out-of-sample testing reduces the risk the forecasting edge is illusory hindsight.
insights.quote: no future inflation data is used in constructing the index
insights.locator: To further evaluate whether the ISMI

insights.claim: The ISMI declines significantly and stays low for an extended period after a contractionary monetary policy shock, consistent with tightening restraining demand and putting sustained downward pressure on inflation.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Confirms the index behaves like real inflation dynamics under policy tightening, supporting its use to read policy transmission.
insights.quote: the ISMI declines significantly and remains low for an extended period in response to a monetary policy shock aimed at reducing inflation
insights.locator: Another way to evaluate the ISMI

insights.claim: The ISMI rises significantly but only transitorily in response to negative news about future oil supply, matching the typically short-lived impact of supply shocks.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: The index distinguishes durable (policy/demand) from transitory (supply) pressures, helping separate noise from signal.
insights.quote: The transitory nature of the response is consistent with the response of other economic variables to typical supply shocks.

insights.claim: As of May 2026 the ISMI has been fluctuating around zero, indicating current elevated inflation readings may persist in the near to medium term rather than clearly rising or falling.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Signals no imminent inflation break in either direction, supporting a hold-near-current-levels base case for rates and inflation trades.
insights.quote: Index readings in 2026 have been fluctuating above and below zero, indicating that inflation may remain near current elevated levels in the near to medium term.

insights.claim: The characterization of 2026 inflation as remaining near 'current elevated levels' implies inflation is still running above the Fed's 2% target as of mid-2026.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: An implied above-target inflation environment persisting through 2026 argues against aggressive dovish repricing.
insights.quote: inflation may remain near current elevated levels

insights.claim: The San Francisco Fed has launched a public data page publishing the ISMI with monthly updates, making the indicator freely trackable in real time.
insights.speaker: Adam Shapiro
insights.so_what: Investors can incorporate the monthly ISMI directly into inflation-nowcasting workflows at no cost.
insights.quote: the San Francisco Fed has launched a new data page for the Inflation Shock Momentum Index with monthly updates
insights.locator: This Economic Letter describes a new indicator
